## Installation

Start a redis via docker:

``` bash
docker run -p 6379:6379 -it redis/redis-stack:latest
```

To install redis-py, simply:

``` bash
$ pip install redis
```

For faster performance, install redis with hiredis support, this provides a compiled response parser, and *for most cases* requires zero code changes.
By default, if hiredis >= 1.0 is available, redis-py will attempt to use it for response parsing.

``` bash
$ pip install "redis[hiredis]"
```

Looking for a high-level library to handle object mapping? See [redis-om-python](https://github.com/redis/redis-om-python)!

The most recent version of this library supports redis version [5.0](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/5.0/00-RELEASENOTES), [6.0](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/6.0/00-RELEASENOTES), [6.2](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/6.2/00-RELEASENOTES), [7.0](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/7.0/00-RELEASENOTES), [7.2](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/7.2/00-RELEASENOTES) and [7.4](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/7.4/00-RELEASENOTES).

import threading
import time as mod_time
import uuid
from types import SimpleNamespace, TracebackType
from typing import Optional, Type

from redis.exceptions import LockError, LockNotOwnedError
from redis.typing import Number


class Lock:
    """
    A shared, distributed Lock. Using Redis for locking allows the Lock
    to be shared across processes and/or machines.

    It's left to the user to resolve deadlock issues and make sure
    multiple clients play nicely together.
    """

    lua_release = None
    lua_extend = None
    lua_reacquire = None

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # return 1 if the lock was released, otherwise 0
    LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - additional milliseconds
    # ARGV[3] - "0" if the additional time should be added to the lock's
    #           existing ttl or "1" if the existing ttl should be replaced
    # return 1 if the locks time was extended, otherwise 0
    LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        local expiration = redis.call('pttl', KEYS[1])
        if not expiration then
            expiration = 0
        end
        if expiration < 0 then
            return 0
        end

        local newttl = ARGV[2]
        if ARGV[3] == "0" then
            newttl = ARGV[2] + expiration
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], newttl)
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - milliseconds
    # return 1 if the locks time was reacquired, otherwise 0
    LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], ARGV[2])
        return 1
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        redis,
        name: str,
        timeout: Optional[Number] = None,
        sleep: Number = 0.1,
        blocking: bool = True,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[Number] = None,
        thread_local: bool = True,
    ):
        """
        Create a new Lock instance named ``name`` using the Redis client
        supplied by ``redis``.

        ``timeout`` indicates a maximum life for the lock in seconds.
        By default, it will remain locked until release() is called.
        ``timeout`` can be specified as a float or integer, both representing
        the number of seconds to wait.

        ``sleep`` indicates the amount of time to sleep in seconds per loop
        iteration when the lock is in blocking mode and another client is
        currently holding the lock.

        ``blocking`` indicates whether calling ``acquire`` should block until
        the lock has been acquired or to fail immediately, causing ``acquire``
        to return False and the lock not being acquired. Defaults to True.
        Note this value can be overridden by passing a ``blocking``
        argument to ``acquire``.

        ``blocking_timeout`` indicates the maximum amount of time in seconds to
        spend trying to acquire the lock. A value of ``None`` indicates
        continue trying forever. ``blocking_timeout`` can be specified as a
        float or integer, both representing the number of seconds to wait.

        ``thread_local`` indicates whether the lock token is placed in
        thread-local storage. By default, the token is placed in thread local
        storage so that a thread only sees its token, not a token set by
        another thread. Consider the following timeline:

            time: 0, thread-1 acquires `my-lock`, with a timeout of 5 seconds.
                     thread-1 sets the token to "abc"
            time: 1, thread-2 blocks trying to acquire `my-lock` using the
                     Lock instance.
            time: 5, thread-1 has not yet completed. redis expires the lock
                     key.
            time: 5, thread-2 acquired `my-lock` now that it's available.
                     thread-2 sets the token to "xyz"
            time: 6, thread-1 finishes its work and calls release(). if the
                     token is *not* stored in thread local storage, then
                     thread-1 would see the token value as "xyz" and would be
                     able to successfully release the thread-2's lock.

        In some use cases it's necessary to disable thread local storage. For
        example, if you have code where one thread acquires a lock and passes
        that lock instance to a worker thread to release later. If thread
        local storage isn't disabled in this case, the worker thread won't see
        the token set by the thread that acquired the lock. Our assumption
        is that these cases aren't common and as such default to using
        thread local storage.
        """
        self.redis = redis
        self.name = name
        self.timeout = timeout
        self.sleep = sleep
        self.blocking = blocking
        self.blocking_timeout = blocking_timeout
        self.thread_local = bool(thread_local)
        self.local = threading.local() if self.thread_local else SimpleNamespace()
        self.local.token = None
        self.register_scripts()

    def register_scripts(self) -> None:
        cls = self.__class__
        client = self.redis
        if cls.lua_release is None:
            cls.lua_release = client.register_script(cls.LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_extend is None:
            cls.lua_extend = client.register_script(cls.LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_reacquire is None:
            cls.lua_reacquire = client.register_script(cls.LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT)

    def __enter__(self) -> "Lock":
        if self.acquire():
            return self
        raise LockError(
            "Unable to acquire lock within the time specified",
            lock_name=self.name,
        )

    def __exit__(
        self,
        exc_type: Optional[Type[BaseException]],
        exc_value: Optional[BaseException],
        traceback: Optional[TracebackType],
    ) -> None:
        self.release()

    def acquire(
        self,
        sleep: Optional[Number] = None,
        blocking: Optional[bool] = None,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[Number] = None,
        token: Optional[str] = None,
    ):
        """
        Use Redis to hold a shared, distributed lock named ``name``.
        Returns True once the lock is acquired.

        If ``blocking`` is False, always return immediately. If the lock
        was acquired, return True, otherwise return False.

        ``blocking_timeout`` specifies the maximum number of seconds to
        wait trying to acquire the lock.

        ``token`` specifies the token value to be used. If provided, token
        must be a bytes object or a string that can be encoded to a bytes
        object with the default encoding. If a token isn't specified, a UUID
        will be generated.
        """
        if sleep is None:
            sleep = self.sleep
        if token is None:
            token = uuid.uuid1().hex.encode()
        else:
            encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            token = encoder.encode(token)
        if blocking is None:
            blocking = self.blocking
        if blocking_timeout is None:
            blocking_timeout = self.blocking_timeout
        stop_trying_at = None
        if blocking_timeout is not None:
            stop_trying_at = mod_time.monotonic() + blocking_timeout
        while True:
            if self.do_acquire(token):
                self.local.token = token
                return True
            if not blocking:
                return False
            next_try_at = mod_time.monotonic() + sleep
            if stop_trying_at is not None and next_try_at > stop_trying_at:
                return False
            mod_time.sleep(sleep)

    def do_acquire(self, token: str) -> bool:
        if self.timeout:
            # convert to milliseconds
            timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        else:
            timeout = None
        if self.redis.set(self.name, token, nx=True, px=timeout):
            return True
        return False

    def locked(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by any process, otherwise False.
        """
        return self.redis.get(self.name) is not None

    def owned(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by this lock, otherwise False.
        """
        stored_token = self.redis.get(self.name)
        # need to always compare bytes to bytes
        # TODO: this can be simplified when the context manager is finished
        if stored_token and not isinstance(stored_token, bytes):
            encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            stored_token = encoder.encode(stored_token)
        return self.local.token is not None and stored_token == self.local.token

    def release(self) -> None:
        """
        Releases the already acquired lock
        """
        expected_token = self.local.token
        if expected_token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot release an unlocked lock", lock_name=self.name)
        self.local.token = None
        self.do_release(expected_token)

    def do_release(self, expected_token: str) -> None:
        if not bool(
            self.lua_release(keys=[self.name], args=[expected_token], client=self.redis)
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError(
                "Cannot release a lock that's no longer owned",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )

    def extend(self, additional_time: Number, replace_ttl: bool = False) -> bool:
        """
        Adds more time to an already acquired lock.

        ``additional_time`` can be specified as an integer or a float, both
        representing the number of seconds to add.

        ``replace_ttl`` if False (the default), add `additional_time` to
        the lock's existing ttl. If True, replace the lock's ttl with
        `additional_time`.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend an unlocked lock", lock_name=self.name)
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend a lock with no timeout", lock_name=self.name)
        return self.do_extend(additional_time, replace_ttl)

    def do_extend(self, additional_time: Number, replace_ttl: bool) -> bool:
        additional_time = int(additional_time * 1000)
        if not bool(
            self.lua_extend(
                keys=[self.name],
                args=[self.local.token, additional_time, "1" if replace_ttl else "0"],
                client=self.redis,
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError(
                "Cannot extend a lock that's no longer owned",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )
        return True

    def reacquire(self) -> bool:
        """
        Resets a TTL of an already acquired lock back to a timeout value.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot reacquire an unlocked lock", lock_name=self.name)
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError(
                "Cannot reacquire a lock with no timeout",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )
        return self.do_reacquire()

    def do_reacquire(self) -> bool:
        timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        if not bool(
            self.lua_reacquire(
                keys=[self.name], args=[self.local.token, timeout], client=self.redis
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError(
                "Cannot reacquire a lock that's no longer owned",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )
        return True

### File Overview
This file implements a distributed Lock class backed by Redis, including Lua scripts for atomic release/extend/reacquire operations and methods to acquire, release, extend, and check lock ownership. It raises LockError and LockNotOwnedError when operations are invalid or ownership is lost.

### Architecture
- Top-level constants and class variables: LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT, LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT, LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT contain Lua code used to perform atomic operations in Redis. Class-level script handles (lua_release, lua_extend, lua_reacquire) are cached on the class.
- The Lock class encapsulates a Redis-backed lock instance. It holds state (redis client, name, timeout, sleep, blocking behavior, thread-local storage for token) and registers the Lua scripts with the Redis client on initialization.
- Public methods implement context-manager behavior (__enter__, __exit__), acquisition (acquire, do_acquire), status checks (locked, owned), release (release, do_release), TTL management (extend, do_extend), and TTL reset (reacquire, do_reacquire).
- The Lua scripts are invoked via the registered script handles with keys and args; failures in these scripts translate into LockNotOwnedError exceptions.

### Functions & Classes
- Lock — class: Implements a Redis-backed distributed lock. Central class in the file; all behaviors (acquire, release, extend, reacquire, ownership checks) are implemented as methods on this class.

- lua_release — class attribute: initially None; holds the registered Lua script handle for release after register_scripts() is called.

- lua_extend — class attribute: initially None; holds the registered Lua script handle for extend after register_scripts() is called.

- lua_reacquire — class attribute: initially None; holds the registered Lua script handle for reacquire after register_scripts() is called.

- LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT — class attribute (string): Lua script that checks the token and deletes the key if token matches. Returns 1 on success, 0 otherwise. Used to perform an atomic release that verifies ownership.

- LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT — class attribute (string): Lua script that checks token ownership, computes new TTL and sets it via pexpire. Returns 1 on success, 0 otherwise. Used to atomically extend TTL only if owned.

- LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT — class attribute (string): Lua script that checks token ownership and sets a new TTL via pexpire. Returns 1 on success, 0 otherwise. Used to reacquire TTL only if owned.

- __init__ — method: Initializes a Lock instance with parameters:
  - redis: the Redis client
  - name: lock name
  - timeout: maximum life in seconds (optional)
  - sleep: sleep interval while blocking
  - blocking: default blocking behavior
  - blocking_timeout: maximum time to block when acquiring
  - thread_local: whether to store token in thread-local storage

import threading
import time as mod_time
import uuid
from types import SimpleNamespace, TracebackType
from typing import Optional, Type

from redis.exceptions import LockError, LockNotOwnedError
from redis.typing import Number


class Lock:
    """
    A shared, distributed Lock. Using Redis for locking allows the Lock
    to be shared across processes and/or machines.

    It's left to the user to resolve deadlock issues and make sure
    multiple clients play nicely together.
    """

    lua_release = None
    lua_extend = None
    lua_reacquire = None

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # return 1 if the lock was released, otherwise 0
    LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - additional milliseconds
    # ARGV[3] - "0" if the additional time should be added to the lock's
    #           existing ttl or "1" if the existing ttl should be replaced
    # return 1 if the locks time was extended, otherwise 0
    LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        local expiration = redis.call('pttl', KEYS[1])
        if not expiration then
            expiration = 0
        end
        if expiration < 0 then
            return 0
        end

        local newttl = ARGV[2]
        if ARGV[3] == "0" then
            newttl = ARGV[2] + expiration
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], newttl)
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - milliseconds
    # return 1 if the locks time was reacquired, otherwise 0
    LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], ARGV[2])
        return 1
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        redis,
        name: str,
        timeout: Optional[Number] = None,
        sleep: Number = 0.1,
        blocking: bool = True,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[Number] = None,
        thread_local: bool = True,
    ):
        """
        Create a new Lock instance named ``name`` using the Redis client
        supplied by ``redis``.

        ``timeout`` indicates a maximum life for the lock in seconds.
        By default, it will remain locked until release() is called.
        ``timeout`` can be specified as a float or integer, both representing
        the number of seconds to wait.

        ``sleep`` indicates the amount of time to sleep in seconds per loop
        iteration when the lock is in blocking mode and another client is
        currently holding the lock.

        ``blocking`` indicates whether calling ``acquire`` should block until
        the lock has been acquired or to fail immediately, causing ``acquire``
        to return False and the lock not being acquired. Defaults to True.
        Note this value can be overridden by passing a ``blocking``
        argument to ``acquire``.

        ``blocking_timeout`` indicates the maximum amount of time in seconds to
        spend trying to acquire the lock. A value of ``None`` indicates
        continue trying forever. ``blocking_timeout`` can be specified as a
        float or integer, both representing the number of seconds to wait.

        ``thread_local`` indicates whether the lock token is placed in
        thread-local storage. By default, the token is placed in thread local
        storage so that a thread only sees its token, not a token set by
        another thread. Consider the following timeline:

            time: 0, thread-1 acquires `my-lock`, with a timeout of 5 seconds.
                     thread-1 sets the token to "abc"
            time: 1, thread-2 blocks trying to acquire `my-lock` using the
                     Lock instance.
            time: 5, thread-1 has not yet completed. redis expires the lock
                     key.
            time: 5, thread-2 acquired `my-lock` now that it's available.
                     thread-2 sets the token to "xyz"
            time: 6, thread-1 finishes its work and calls release(). if the
                     token is *not* stored in thread local storage, then
                     thread-1 would see the token value as "xyz" and would be
                     able to successfully release the thread-2's lock.

        In some use cases it's necessary to disable thread local storage. For
        example, if you have code where one thread acquires a lock and passes
        that lock instance to a worker thread to release later. If thread
        local storage isn't disabled in this case, the worker thread won't see
        the token set by the thread that acquired the lock. Our assumption
        is that these cases aren't common and as such default to using
        thread local storage.
        """
        self.redis = redis
        self.name = name
        self.timeout = timeout
        self.sleep = sleep
        self.blocking = blocking
        self.blocking_timeout = blocking_timeout
        self.thread_local = bool(thread_local)
        self.local = threading.local() if self.thread_local else SimpleNamespace()
        self.local.token = None
        self.register_scripts()

    def register_scripts(self) -> None:
        cls = self.__class__
        client = self.redis
        if cls.lua_release is None:
            cls.lua_release = client.register_script(cls.LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_extend is None:
            cls.lua_extend = client.register_script(cls.LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_reacquire is None:
            cls.lua_reacquire = client.register_script(cls.LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT)

    def __enter__(self) -> "Lock":
        if self.acquire():
            return self
        raise LockError(
            "Unable to acquire lock within the time specified",
            lock_name=self.name,
        )

    def __exit__(
        self,
        exc_type: Optional[Type[BaseException]],
        exc_value: Optional[BaseException],
        traceback: Optional[TracebackType],
    ) -> None:
        self.release()

    def acquire(
        self,
        sleep: Optional[Number] = None,
        blocking: Optional[bool] = None,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[Number] = None,
        token: Optional[str] = None,
    ):
        """
        Use Redis to hold a shared, distributed lock named ``name``.
        Returns True once the lock is acquired.

        If ``blocking`` is False, always return immediately. If the lock
        was acquired, return True, otherwise return False.

        ``blocking_timeout`` specifies the maximum number of seconds to
        wait trying to acquire the lock.

        ``token`` specifies the token value to be used. If provided, token
        must be a bytes object or a string that can be encoded to a bytes
        object with the default encoding. If a token isn't specified, a UUID
        will be generated.
        """
        if sleep is None:
            sleep = self.sleep
        if token is None:
            token = uuid.uuid1().hex.encode()
        else:
            encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            token = encoder.encode(token)
        if blocking is None:
            blocking = self.blocking
        if blocking_timeout is None:
            blocking_timeout = self.blocking_timeout
        stop_trying_at = None
        if blocking_timeout is not None:
            stop_trying_at = mod_time.monotonic() + blocking_timeout
        while True:
            if self.do_acquire(token):
                self.local.token = token
                return True
            if not blocking:
                return False
            next_try_at = mod_time.monotonic() + sleep
            if stop_trying_at is not None and next_try_at > stop_trying_at:
                return False
            mod_time.sleep(sleep)

    def do_acquire(self, token: str) -> bool:
        if self.timeout:
            # convert to milliseconds
            timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        else:
            timeout = None
        if self.redis.set(self.name, token, nx=True, px=timeout):
            return True
        return False

    def locked(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by any process, otherwise False.
        """
        return self.redis.get(self.name) is not None

    def owned(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by this lock, otherwise False.
        """
        stored_token = self.redis.get(self.name)
        # need to always compare bytes to bytes
        # TODO: this can be simplified when the context manager is finished
        if stored_token and not isinstance(stored_token, bytes):
            encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            stored_token = encoder.encode(stored_token)
        return self.local.token is not None and stored_token == self.local.token

    def release(self) -> None:
        """
        Releases the already acquired lock
        """
        expected_token = self.local.token
        if expected_token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot release an unlocked lock", lock_name=self.name)
        self.local.token = None
        self.do_release(expected_token)

    def do_release(self, expected_token: str) -> None:
        if not bool(
            self.lua_release(keys=[self.name], args=[expected_token], client=self.redis)
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError(
                "Cannot release a lock that's no longer owned",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )

    def extend(self, additional_time: Number, replace_ttl: bool = False) -> bool:
        """
        Adds more time to an already acquired lock.

        ``additional_time`` can be specified as an integer or a float, both
        representing the number of seconds to add.

        ``replace_ttl`` if False (the default), add `additional_time` to
        the lock's existing ttl. If True, replace the lock's ttl with
        `additional_time`.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend an unlocked lock", lock_name=self.name)
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend a lock with no timeout", lock_name=self.name)
        return self.do_extend(additional_time, replace_ttl)

    def do_extend(self, additional_time: Number, replace_ttl: bool) -> bool:
        additional_time = int(additional_time * 1000)
        if not bool(
            self.lua_extend(
                keys=[self.name],
                args=[self.local.token, additional_time, "1" if replace_ttl else "0"],
                client=self.redis,
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError(
                "Cannot extend a lock that's no longer owned",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )
        return True

    def reacquire(self) -> bool:
        """
        Resets a TTL of an already acquired lock back to a timeout value.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot reacquire an unlocked lock", lock_name=self.name)
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError(
                "Cannot reacquire a lock with no timeout",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )
        return self.do_reacquire()

    def do_reacquire(self) -> bool:
        timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        if not bool(
            self.lua_reacquire(
                keys=[self.name], args=[self.local.token, timeout], client=self.redis
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError(
                "Cannot reacquire a lock that's no longer owned",
                lock_name=self.name,
            )
        return True

import time

import pytest
from redis.client import Redis
from redis.exceptions import LockError, LockNotOwnedError
from redis.lock import Lock

from .conftest import _get_client


class TestLock:
    @pytest.fixture()
    def r_decoded(self, request):
        return _get_client(Redis, request=request, decode_responses=True)

    def get_lock(self, redis, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs["lock_class"] = Lock
        return redis.lock(*args, **kwargs)

    def test_lock(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo")
        assert lock.acquire(blocking=False)
        assert r.get("foo") == lock.local.token
        assert r.ttl("foo") == -1
        lock.release()
        assert r.get("foo") is None

    def test_lock_token(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo")
        self._test_lock_token(r, lock)

    def test_lock_token_thread_local_false(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo", thread_local=False)
        self._test_lock_token(r, lock)

    def _test_lock_token(self, r, lock):
        assert lock.acquire(blocking=False, token="test")
        assert r.get("foo") == b"test"

### File Overview
This file contains pytest test cases for redis.lock.Lock behavior (acquire/release/extend/reacquire, context-manager behavior, ownership checks, timeouts). It verifies correct exceptions (LockError, LockNotOwnedError), token handling, TTL/pttl semantics, and lock class selection.

### Architecture
- Two test classes: TestLock (comprehensive locking behavior) and TestLockClassSelection (lock_class injection).
- Tests use a helper fixture r_decoded and a helper method get_lock to construct Lock instances using the real Lock class via redis.lock.Lock.
- Tests interact with a Redis client provided by external fixtures (r and r_decoded from conftest/_get_client), directly asserting Redis keys, TTLs, and exception behavior.
- Most tests follow the pattern: create lock via get_lock, call Lock methods (acquire/release/extend/reacquire/locked/owned), then assert Redis state and raised exceptions.

### Functions & Classes
- TestLock — container class grouping many lock behavior tests and helpers.

  - r_decoded — pytest.fixture()  
    Returns: _get_client(Redis, request=request, decode_responses=True).  
    Importance: Provides a Redis client with decode_responses=True for tests that need decoded responses (bytes vs str behavior).

  - get_lock(self, redis, *args, **kwargs)  
    Sets kwargs["lock_class"] = Lock and returns redis.lock(*args, **kwargs).  
    Importance: Centralizes lock construction to ensure tests use redis.lock.Lock explicitly and to pass through other lock parameters (timeout, blocking, etc.).

  - test_lock(self, r)  
    Verifies basic acquire/release: acquire(blocking=False) sets key to lock.local.token, TTL is -1 (no expire), release deletes key.  
    Importance: Basic sanity check for Lock.acquire/release and key removal.

  - test_lock_token(self, r)  
    Delegates to _test_lock_token using default thread_local behavior.  
    Importance: Ensures token propagation works.

  - test_lock_token_thread_local_false(self, r)  
    Delegates to _test_lock_token with thread_local=False.  
    Importance: Ensures token handling when thread_local is disabled.

  - _test_lock_token(self, r, lock)  
    Acquires lock with token="test", asserts Redis key equals b"test", local.token matches b"test", TTL -1, release clears key and local.token.  
    Importance: Verifies explicit token passing and cleanup behavior.

  - test_locked(self, r)  
    Asserts locked() toggles correctly before/after acquire and release.  
    Importance: locked() correctness.

  - _test_owned(self, client)  
    Exercises owned() in several scenarios (not owned, owned after acquire, two locks competing).  
    Importance: Shared helper to validate owned() behavior for different client configurations.

  - test_owned(self, r)  
    Calls _test_owned with r.  
    Importance: Verifies owned() on standard client.

  - test_owned_with_decoded_responses(self, r_decoded)  
    Calls _test_owned with r_decoded.  
    Importance: Verifies owned() when responses are decoded.

  - test_competing_locks(self, r)  
    Ensures two locks on same key cannot both acquire; release flips ownership.  
    Importance: Mutual exclusion correctness.

  - test_timeout(self, r)  
    lock with timeout=10 sets TTL approximately in that range.  
    Importance: Verifies expire semantics.

  - test_float_timeout(self, r)  
    timeout as float and use of pttl for milliseconds.  
    Importance: Millisecond precision behavior.

  - test_blocking_timeout(self, r)  
    Tests blocking acquisition with blocking_timeout and sleep settings and validates elapsed time constraints.  
    Importance: Blocking acquisition timing behavior.

  - test_context_manager(self, r)  
    Using Lock as context manager acquires and automatically releases; asserts key removal at exit.  
    Importance: Context-manager semantics and automatic release.

  - test_context_manager_blocking_timeout(self, r)  
    Nested context manager and blocking_timeout timing test similar to test_blocking_timeout.  
    Importance: Ensures context manager respects blocking behavior.

  - test_context_manager_raises_when_locked_not_acquired(self, r)  
    Sets the key manually (r.set("foo","bar")) then with pytest.raises(LockError): with self.get_lock(..., blocking_timeout=0.1): pass  
    Importance: Verifies context-manager raises LockError when it cannot acquire lock due to existing key.

  - test_high_sleep_small_blocking_timeout(self, r)

  - test_releasing_unlocked_lock_raises_error(self, r)

  - test_reacquiring_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r)  
    Acquire lock created without timeout and assert reacquire raises LockError.  
    Importance: reacquire() requires original timeout.

  - test_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r)  
    Acquire with timeout, mutate key, then with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError): lock.reacquire()  
    Importance: reacquire() checks ownership.

  - test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r)  
    Uses context manager with timeout=None and blocking=False then inside with pytest.raises(LockError): lock.reacquire()  
    Importance: Context-managed reacquire behavior when no timeout set.

  - test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r)  
    with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError): with self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10, blocking=False): r.set("foo", "a")  
    Importance: Ensures that if lock is lost while inside context manager, the LockNotOwnedError propagates (context-manager release detects non-ownership).

  - test_lock_error_gives_correct_lock_name(self, r)  
    r.set("foo", "bar"), then with pytest.raises(LockError) as excinfo: with self.get_lock(r, "foo", blocking_timeout=0.1): pass; assert excinfo.value.lock_name == "foo"  
    Importance: Directly relevant to agent's goal — checks that LockError includes lock_name attribute set to the correct lock key. Note: this test asserts an attribute on the exception (lock_name), not the textual exception message.

- TestLockClassSelection — container for lock class selection test.

  - test_lock_class_argument(self, r)  
    Defines inner class MyLock with __init__ and then does lock = r.lock("foo", lock_class=MyLock); assert isinstance(lock, MyLock)  
    Importance: Verifies r.lock honors the lock_class argument and returns instance of provided class.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- Tests directly asserting exception types for release/ownership failures:
  - test_releasing_unlocked_lock_raises_error — asserts LockError on releasing an unlocked lock.
  - test_releasing_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error — asserts LockNotOwnedError when token no longer matches and also checks that lock.local.token is cleared after the exception.
  - test_extending_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error and test_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error — similar ownership-related exception checks for extend/reacquire.
  - test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error and test_context_manager_raises_when_locked_not_acquired — context-manager cases that raise LockNotOwnedError or LockError.

This is the only test asserting an attribute on the raised exception; there are no tests asserting on exact textual error messages. If your PR needs to change exception message strings, note that these tests do not validate message text, only exception types and the lock_name attribute.

        assert 8000 < r.pttl("foo") <= 10000
        assert lock.extend(10.0)
        assert 16000 < r.pttl("foo") <= 20000
        lock.release()

    def test_extending_unlocked_lock_raises_error(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10)
        with pytest.raises(LockError):
            lock.extend(10)

    def test_extending_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo")
        assert lock.acquire(blocking=False)
        with pytest.raises(LockError):
            lock.extend(10)
        lock.release()

    def test_extending_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10)
        assert lock.acquire(blocking=False)
        r.set("foo", "a")
        with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError):
            lock.extend(10)

    def test_reacquire_lock(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10)
        assert lock.acquire(blocking=False)
        assert r.pexpire("foo", 5000)
        assert r.pttl("foo") <= 5000
        assert lock.reacquire()
        assert 8000 < r.pttl("foo") <= 10000
        lock.release()

    def test_reacquiring_unlocked_lock_raises_error(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10)
        with pytest.raises(LockError):
            lock.reacquire()

    def test_reacquiring_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo")
        assert lock.acquire(blocking=False)
        with pytest.raises(LockError):
            lock.reacquire()
        lock.release()

    def test_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r):
        lock = self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10)
        assert lock.acquire(blocking=False)
        r.set("foo", "a")
        with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError):
            lock.reacquire()

    def test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r):
        with self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=None, blocking=False) as lock:
            with pytest.raises(LockError):
                lock.reacquire()

    def test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r):
        with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError):
            with self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10, blocking=False):
                r.set("foo", "a")

    def test_lock_error_gives_correct_lock_name(self, r):
        r.set("foo", "bar")
        with pytest.raises(LockError) as excinfo:
            with self.get_lock(r, "foo", blocking_timeout=0.1):
                pass
            assert excinfo.value.lock_name == "foo"


class TestLockClassSelection:
    def test_lock_class_argument(self, r):
        class MyLock:
            def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
                pass

        lock = r.lock("foo", lock_class=MyLock)
        assert isinstance(lock, MyLock)

### File Overview
This file contains pytest test cases for redis.lock.Lock behavior (acquire/release/extend/reacquire, context-manager behavior, ownership checks, timeouts). It verifies correct exceptions (LockError, LockNotOwnedError), token handling, TTL/pttl semantics, and lock class selection.

### Architecture
- Two test classes: TestLock (comprehensive locking behavior) and TestLockClassSelection (lock_class injection).
- Tests use a helper fixture r_decoded and a helper method get_lock to construct Lock instances using the real Lock class via redis.lock.Lock.
- Tests interact with a Redis client provided by external fixtures (r and r_decoded from conftest/_get_client), directly asserting Redis keys, TTLs, and exception behavior.
- Most tests follow the pattern: create lock via get_lock, call Lock methods (acquire/release/extend/reacquire/locked/owned), then assert Redis state and raised exceptions.

### Functions & Classes
- TestLock — container class grouping many lock behavior tests and helpers.

  - r_decoded — pytest.fixture()  
    Returns: _get_client(Redis, request=request, decode_responses=True).  
    Importance: Provides a Redis client with decode_responses=True for tests that need decoded responses (bytes vs str behavior).

  - get_lock(self, redis, *args, **kwargs)  
    Sets kwargs["lock_class"] = Lock and returns redis.lock(*args, **kwargs).  
    Importance: Centralizes lock construction to ensure tests use redis.lock.Lock explicitly and to pass through other lock parameters (timeout, blocking, etc.).

  - test_lock(self, r)  
    Verifies basic acquire/release: acquire(blocking=False) sets key to lock.local.token, TTL is -1 (no expire), release deletes key.  
    Importance: Basic sanity check for Lock.acquire/release and key removal.

  - test_lock_token(self, r)  
    Delegates to _test_lock_token using default thread_local behavior.  
    Importance: Ensures token propagation works.

  - test_lock_token_thread_local_false(self, r)  
    Delegates to _test_lock_token with thread_local=False.  
    Importance: Ensures token handling when thread_local is disabled.

  - _test_lock_token(self, r, lock)  
    Acquires lock with token="test", asserts Redis key equals b"test", local.token matches b"test", TTL -1, release clears key and local.token.  
    Importance: Verifies explicit token passing and cleanup behavior.

  - test_locked(self, r)  
    Asserts locked() toggles correctly before/after acquire and release.  
    Importance: locked() correctness.

  - _test_owned(self, client)  
    Exercises owned() in several scenarios (not owned, owned after acquire, two locks competing).  
    Importance: Shared helper to validate owned() behavior for different client configurations.

  - test_owned(self, r)  
    Calls _test_owned with r.  
    Importance: Verifies owned() on standard client.

  - test_owned_with_decoded_responses(self, r_decoded)  
    Calls _test_owned with r_decoded.  
    Importance: Verifies owned() when responses are decoded.

  - test_competing_locks(self, r)  
    Ensures two locks on same key cannot both acquire; release flips ownership.  
    Importance: Mutual exclusion correctness.

  - test_timeout(self, r)  
    lock with timeout=10 sets TTL approximately in that range.  
    Importance: Verifies expire semantics.

  - test_float_timeout(self, r)  
    timeout as float and use of pttl for milliseconds.  
    Importance: Millisecond precision behavior.

  - test_blocking_timeout(self, r)  
    Tests blocking acquisition with blocking_timeout and sleep settings and validates elapsed time constraints.  
    Importance: Blocking acquisition timing behavior.

  - test_context_manager(self, r)  
    Using Lock as context manager acquires and automatically releases; asserts key removal at exit.  
    Importance: Context-manager semantics and automatic release.

  - test_context_manager_blocking_timeout(self, r)  
    Nested context manager and blocking_timeout timing test similar to test_blocking_timeout.  
    Importance: Ensures context manager respects blocking behavior.

  - test_context_manager_raises_when_locked_not_acquired(self, r)  
    Sets the key manually (r.set("foo","bar")) then with pytest.raises(LockError): with self.get_lock(..., blocking_timeout=0.1): pass  
    Importance: Verifies context-manager raises LockError when it cannot acquire lock due to existing key.

  - test_high_sleep_small_blocking_timeout(self, r)  
    Configures a very high sleep and small blocking_timeout; asserts acquire() returns quickly (unattainable) and elapsed time less than blocking_timeout.  
    Importance: Edge-case blocking/sleep interaction.

  - test_releasing_unlocked_lock_raises_error(self, r)  
    Creates a lock but does not acquire it; with pytest.raises(LockError): lock.release()  
    Importance: Verifies that releasing a lock that was never acquired raises LockError.

  - test_releasing_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r)  
    Acquires lock then manually mutates Redis key (r.set("foo", "a")) to simulate foreign ownership; with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError): lock.release()  
    Afterwards asserts lock.local.token is None even though release raised.  
    Importance: Critical for the agent's goal — asserts LockNotOwnedError is raised when token mismatch occurs and that the local token is cleared after failed release.

  - test_extend_lock(self, r)  
    Acquire with timeout, check pttl range, call extend(10) to increase TTL, assert new pttl range, release.  
    Importance: extend() semantics when timeout present.

  - test_extend_lock_replace_ttl(self, r)  
    extend(..., replace_ttl=True) should reset TTL to original timeout instead of adding.  
    Importance: replace_ttl option behavior.

  - test_extend_lock_float(self, r)  
    extend with float timeout; verifies pttl updated accordingly.  
    Importance: Millisecond precision for extend().

  - test_extending_unlocked_lock_raises_error(self, r)  
    extend called on an unlocked lock raises LockError.  
    Importance: Guarding extend() preconditions.

  - test_extending_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r)  
    Acquire a lock created without timeout and assert extend raises LockError.  
    Importance: extend() requires original timeout.

  - test_extending_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r)  
    Acquire with timeout then mutate Redis key; with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError): lock.extend(10)  
    Importance: extend() respects ownership and raises LockNotOwnedError on mismatch.

  - test_reacquire_lock(self, r)  
    Acquire with timeout, manually shorten expiry via pexpire, then call reacquire() to restore original timeout; assert pttl range.  
    Importance: reacquire() semantics.

  - test_reacquiring_unlocked_lock_raises_error(self, r)  
    reacquire() on unlocked lock raises LockError.  
    Importance: Guarding reacquire() usage.

  - test_reacquiring_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r)  
    Acquire lock created without timeout and assert reacquire raises LockError.  
    Importance: reacquire() requires original timeout.

  - test_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r)  
    Acquire with timeout, mutate key, then with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError): lock.reacquire()  
    Importance: reacquire() checks ownership.

  - test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_with_no_timeout_raises_error(self, r)  
    Uses context manager with timeout=None and blocking=False then inside with pytest.raises(LockError): lock.reacquire()  
    Importance: Context-managed reacquire behavior when no timeout set.

  - test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error(self, r)  
    with pytest.raises(LockNotOwnedError): with self.get_lock(r, "foo", timeout=10, blocking=False): r.set("foo", "a")  
    Importance: Ensures that if lock is lost while inside context manager, the LockNotOwnedError propagates (context-manager release detects non-ownership).

  - test_lock_error_gives_correct_lock_name(self, r)  
    r.set("foo", "bar"), then with pytest.raises(LockError) as excinfo: with self.get_lock(r, "foo", blocking_timeout=0.1): pass; assert excinfo.value.lock_name == "foo"  
    Importance: Directly relevant to agent's goal — checks that LockError includes lock_name attribute set to the correct lock key. Note: this test asserts an attribute on the exception (lock_name), not the textual exception message.

- TestLockClassSelection — container for lock class selection test.

  - test_lock_class_argument(self, r)  
    Defines inner class MyLock with __init__ and then does lock = r.lock("foo", lock_class=MyLock); assert isinstance(lock, MyLock)  
    Importance: Verifies r.lock honors the lock_class argument and returns instance of provided class.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- Tests directly asserting exception types for release/ownership failures:
  - test_releasing_unlocked_lock_raises_error — asserts LockError on releasing an unlocked lock.
  - test_releasing_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error — asserts LockNotOwnedError when token no longer matches and also checks that lock.local.token is cleared after the exception.
  - test_extending_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error and test_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error — similar ownership-related exception checks for extend/reacquire.
  - test_context_manager_reacquiring_lock_no_longer_owned_raises_error and test_context_manager_raises_when_locked_not_acquired — context-manager cases that raise LockNotOwnedError or LockError.

- Tests inspecting exception content:
  - test_lock_error_gives_correct_lock_name — asserts that the LockError exception exposes a lock_name attribute equal to the key ("foo"). This is the only test asserting an attribute on the raised exception; there are no tests asserting on exact textual error messages. If your PR needs to change exception message strings, note that these tests do not validate message text, only exception types and the lock_name attribute.

- Multithreading: No multithreaded tests nor any reference to issue #3535 are present in this file. If you expected multithreaded coverage for release behavior or a test referencing issue #3535, it is not in this file.

import asyncio
import threading
import uuid
from types import SimpleNamespace
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Awaitable, Optional, Union

from redis.exceptions import LockError, LockNotOwnedError

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from redis.asyncio import Redis, RedisCluster


class Lock:
    """
    A shared, distributed Lock. Using Redis for locking allows the Lock
    to be shared across processes and/or machines.

    It's left to the user to resolve deadlock issues and make sure
    multiple clients play nicely together.
    """

    lua_release = None
    lua_extend = None
    lua_reacquire = None

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # return 1 if the lock was released, otherwise 0
    LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - additional milliseconds
    # ARGV[3] - "0" if the additional time should be added to the lock's
    #           existing ttl or "1" if the existing ttl should be replaced
    # return 1 if the locks time was extended, otherwise 0
    LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        local expiration = redis.call('pttl', KEYS[1])
        if not expiration then
            expiration = 0
        end
        if expiration < 0 then
            return 0
        end

        local newttl = ARGV[2]
        if ARGV[3] == "0" then
            newttl = ARGV[2] + expiration
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], newttl)
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - milliseconds
    # return 1 if the locks time was reacquired, otherwise 0
    LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], ARGV[2])
        return 1
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        redis: Union["Redis", "RedisCluster"],
        name: Union[str, bytes, memoryview],
        timeout: Optional[float] = None,
        sleep: float = 0.1,
        blocking: bool = True,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[float] = None,
        thread_local: bool = True,
    ):
        """
        Create a new Lock instance named ``name`` using the Redis client
        supplied by ``redis``.

        ``timeout`` indicates a maximum life for the lock in seconds.
        By default, it will remain locked until release() is called.
        ``timeout`` can be specified as a float or integer, both representing
        the number of seconds to wait.

        ``sleep`` indicates the amount of time to sleep in seconds per loop
        iteration when the lock is in blocking mode and another client is
        currently holding the lock.

        ``blocking`` indicates whether calling ``acquire`` should block until
        the lock has been acquired or to fail immediately, causing ``acquire``
        to return False and the lock not being acquired. Defaults to True.
        Note this value can be overridden by passing a ``blocking``
        argument to ``acquire``.

        ``blocking_timeout`` indicates the maximum amount of time in seconds to
        spend trying to acquire the lock. A value of ``None`` indicates
        continue trying forever. ``blocking_timeout`` can be specified as a
        float or integer, both representing the number of seconds to wait.

        ``thread_local`` indicates whether the lock token is placed in
        thread-local storage. By default, the token is placed in thread local
        storage so that a thread only sees its token, not a token set by
        another thread. Consider the following timeline:

            time: 0, thread-1 acquires `my-lock`, with a timeout of 5 seconds.
                     thread-1 sets the token to "abc"
            time: 1, thread-2 blocks trying to acquire `my-lock` using the
                     Lock instance.
            time: 5, thread-1 has not yet completed. redis expires the lock
                     key.
            time: 5, thread-2 acquired `my-lock` now that it's available.
                     thread-2 sets the token to "xyz"
            time: 6, thread-1 finishes its work and calls release(). if the
                     token is *not* stored in thread local storage, then
                     thread-1 would see the token value as "xyz" and would be
                     able to successfully release the thread-2's lock.

        In some use cases it's necessary to disable thread local storage. For
        example, if you have code where one thread acquires a lock and passes
        that lock instance to a worker thread to release later. If thread
        local storage isn't disabled in this case, the worker thread won't see
        the token set by the thread that acquired the lock. Our assumption
        is that these cases aren't common and as such default to using
        thread local storage.
        """
        self.redis = redis
        self.name = name
        self.timeout = timeout
        self.sleep = sleep
        self.blocking = blocking
        self.blocking_timeout = blocking_timeout
        self.thread_local = bool(thread_local)
        self.local = threading.local() if self.thread_local else SimpleNamespace()
        self.local.token = None
        self.register_scripts()

    def register_scripts(self):
        cls = self.__class__
        client = self.redis
        if cls.lua_release is None:
            cls.lua_release = client.register_script(cls.LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_extend is None:
            cls.lua_extend = client.register_script(cls.LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_reacquire is None:
            cls.lua_reacquire = client.register_script(cls.LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT)

    async def __aenter__(self):
        if await self.acquire():
            return self
        raise LockError("Unable to acquire lock within the time specified")

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):
        await self.release()

    async def acquire(
        self,
        blocking: Optional[bool] = None,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[float] = None,
        token: Optional[Union[str, bytes]] = None,
    ):
        """
        Use Redis to hold a shared, distributed lock named ``name``.
        Returns True once the lock is acquired.

        If ``blocking`` is False, always return immediately. If the lock
        was acquired, return True, otherwise return False.

        ``blocking_timeout`` specifies the maximum number of seconds to
        wait trying to acquire the lock.

        ``token`` specifies the token value to be used. If provided, token
        must be a bytes object or a string that can be encoded to a bytes
        object with the default encoding. If a token isn't specified, a UUID
        will be generated.
        """
        sleep = self.sleep
        if token is None:
            token = uuid.uuid1().hex.encode()
        else:
            try:
                encoder = self.redis.connection_pool.get_encoder()
            except AttributeError:
                # Cluster
                encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            token = encoder.encode(token)
        if blocking is None:
            blocking = self.blocking
        if blocking_timeout is None:
            blocking_timeout = self.blocking_timeout
        stop_trying_at = None
        if blocking_timeout is not None:
            stop_trying_at = asyncio.get_running_loop().time() + blocking_timeout
        while True:
            if await self.do_acquire(token):
                self.local.token = token
                return True
            if not blocking:
                return False
            next_try_at = asyncio.get_running_loop().time() + sleep
            if stop_trying_at is not None and next_try_at > stop_trying_at:
                return False
            await asyncio.sleep(sleep)

    async def do_acquire(self, token: Union[str, bytes]) -> bool:
        if self.timeout:
            # convert to milliseconds
            timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        else:
            timeout = None
        if await self.redis.set(self.name, token, nx=True, px=timeout):
            return True
        return False

    async def locked(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by any process, otherwise False.
        """
        return await self.redis.get(self.name) is not None

    async def owned(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by this lock, otherwise False.
        """
        stored_token = await self.redis.get(self.name)
        # need to always compare bytes to bytes
        # TODO: this can be simplified when the context manager is finished
        if stored_token and not isinstance(stored_token, bytes):
            try:
                encoder = self.redis.connection_pool.get_encoder()
            except AttributeError:
                # Cluster
                encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            stored_token = encoder.encode(stored_token)
        return self.local.token is not None and stored_token == self.local.token

    def release(self) -> Awaitable[None]:
        """Releases the already acquired lock"""
        expected_token = self.local.token
        if expected_token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot release an unlocked lock")
        self.local.token = None
        return self.do_release(expected_token)

    async def do_release(self, expected_token: bytes) -> None:
        if not bool(
            await self.lua_release(
                keys=[self.name], args=[expected_token], client=self.redis
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError("Cannot release a lock that's no longer owned")

    def extend(
        self, additional_time: float, replace_ttl: bool = False
    ) -> Awaitable[bool]:
        """
        Adds more time to an already acquired lock.

        ``additional_time`` can be specified as an integer or a float, both
        representing the number of seconds to add.

        ``replace_ttl`` if False (the default), add `additional_time` to
        the lock's existing ttl. If True, replace the lock's ttl with
        `additional_time`.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend an unlocked lock")
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend a lock with no timeout")
        return self.do_extend(additional_time, replace_ttl)

    async def do_extend(self, additional_time, replace_ttl) -> bool:
        additional_time = int(additional_time * 1000)
        if not bool(
            await self.lua_extend(
                keys=[self.name],
                args=[self.local.token, additional_time, replace_ttl and "1" or "0"],
                client=self.redis,
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError("Cannot extend a lock that's no longer owned")
        return True

    def reacquire(self) -> Awaitable[bool]:
        """
        Resets a TTL of an already acquired lock back to a timeout value.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot reacquire an unlocked lock")
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot reacquire a lock with no timeout")
        return self.do_reacquire()

    async def do_reacquire(self) -> bool:
        timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        if not bool(
            await self.lua_reacquire(
                keys=[self.name], args=[self.local.token, timeout], client=self.redis
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError("Cannot reacquire a lock that's no longer owned")
        return True

### File Overview
This file implements an asyncio-compatible distributed Lock backed by Redis. It provides async acquire/release semantics, TTL management (extend/reacquire), and uses Lua scripts to perform atomic release/extend/reacquire operations on the Redis server.

### Architecture
- The main component is the class Lock which encapsulates lock state (name, timeout, token storage), Lua scripts for atomic server-side operations, and methods for acquiring/releasing/extending/reacquiring the lock.
- Lock stores a per-instance token in thread-local storage (self.local.token) unless thread_local is disabled; the token is the client-side proof of ownership used in Lua scripts.
- register_scripts registers three Lua scripts (LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT, LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT, LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT) with the Redis client so the class can call them atomically via client-side script objects (lua_release, lua_extend, lua_reacquire).
- acquire/do_acquire handle lock acquisition using SET NX PX; owned/locked/do_* methods interact with Redis via GET or the registered Lua scripts.
- Async context manager support is provided by __aenter__ / __aexit__.

### Functions & Classes
- Lock — main class implementing the distributed lock.
  - Attributes:
    - lua_release, lua_extend, lua_reacquire — class-level holders for registered script objects (initially None).
    - LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT — Lua script that verifies the token matches and deletes the key; returns 1 if released, otherwise 0.
    - LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT — Lua script that verifies the token and extends or replaces the TTL; returns 1 if extended, otherwise 0.
    - LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT — Lua script that verifies the token and sets the TTL; returns 1 if successful, otherwise 0.
  - __init__(self, redis: Union["Redis", "RedisCluster"], name: Union[str, bytes, memoryview], timeout: Optional[float] = None, sleep: float = 0.1, blocking: bool = True, blocking_timeout: Optional[float] = None, thread_local: bool = True)
    - Initializes Lock state: Redis client, lock name, timeout/sleep/blocking semantics, thread-local storage for token, and calls register_scripts().
    - Important because it sets up token storage and ensures scripts are registered for atomic server operations.
  - register_scripts(self)
    - Registers LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT, LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT, and LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT with the Redis client (once per class).
    - Important to prepare lua_release/lua_extend/lua_reacquire to be callable in do_release/do_extend/do_reacquire.
  - async def __aenter__(self)
    - Attempts to acquire the lock via await self.acquire() and returns self on success.
    - Raises LockError with message "Unable to acquire lock within the time specified" if acquire() fails.

## Source Code:
import asyncio
import threading
import uuid
from types import SimpleNamespace
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Awaitable, Optional, Union

from redis.exceptions import LockError, LockNotOwnedError

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from redis.asyncio import Redis, RedisCluster


class Lock:
    """
    A shared, distributed Lock. Using Redis for locking allows the Lock
    to be shared across processes and/or machines.

    It's left to the user to resolve deadlock issues and make sure
    multiple clients play nicely together.
    """

    lua_release = None
    lua_extend = None
    lua_reacquire = None

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # return 1 if the lock was released, otherwise 0
    LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - additional milliseconds
    # ARGV[3] - "0" if the additional time should be added to the lock's
    #           existing ttl or "1" if the existing ttl should be replaced
    # return 1 if the locks time was extended, otherwise 0
    LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        local expiration = redis.call('pttl', KEYS[1])
        if not expiration then
            expiration = 0
        end
        if expiration < 0 then
            return 0
        end

        local newttl = ARGV[2]
        if ARGV[3] == "0" then
            newttl = ARGV[2] + expiration
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], newttl)
        return 1
    """

    # KEYS[1] - lock name
    # ARGV[1] - token
    # ARGV[2] - milliseconds
    # return 1 if the locks time was reacquired, otherwise 0
    LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT = """
        local token = redis.call('get', KEYS[1])
        if not token or token ~= ARGV[1] then
            return 0
        end
        redis.call('pexpire', KEYS[1], ARGV[2])
        return 1
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        redis: Union["Redis", "RedisCluster"],
        name: Union[str, bytes, memoryview],
        timeout: Optional[float] = None,
        sleep: float = 0.1,
        blocking: bool = True,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[float] = None,
        thread_local: bool = True,
    ):
        """
        Create a new Lock instance named ``name`` using the Redis client
        supplied by ``redis``.

        ``timeout`` indicates a maximum life for the lock in seconds.
        By default, it will remain locked until release() is called.
        ``timeout`` can be specified as a float or integer, both representing
        the number of seconds to wait.

        ``sleep`` indicates the amount of time to sleep in seconds per loop
        iteration when the lock is in blocking mode and another client is
        currently holding the lock.

        ``blocking`` indicates whether calling ``acquire`` should block until
        the lock has been acquired or to fail immediately, causing ``acquire``
        to return False and the lock not being acquired. Defaults to True.
        Note this value can be overridden by passing a ``blocking``
        argument to ``acquire``.

        ``blocking_timeout`` indicates the maximum amount of time in seconds to
        spend trying to acquire the lock. A value of ``None`` indicates
        continue trying forever. ``blocking_timeout`` can be specified as a
        float or integer, both representing the number of seconds to wait.

        ``thread_local`` indicates whether the lock token is placed in
        thread-local storage. By default, the token is placed in thread local
        storage so that a thread only sees its token, not a token set by
        another thread. Consider the following timeline:

            time: 0, thread-1 acquires `my-lock`, with a timeout of 5 seconds.
                     thread-1 sets the token to "abc"
            time: 1, thread-2 blocks trying to acquire `my-lock` using the
                     Lock instance.
            time: 5, thread-1 has not yet completed. redis expires the lock
                     key.
            time: 5, thread-2 acquired `my-lock` now that it's available.
                     thread-2 sets the token to "xyz"
            time: 6, thread-1 finishes its work and calls release(). if the
                     token is *not* stored in thread local storage, then
                     thread-1 would see the token value as "xyz" and would be
                     able to successfully release the thread-2's lock.

        In some use cases it's necessary to disable thread local storage. For
        example, if you have code where one thread acquires a lock and passes
        that lock instance to a worker thread to release later. If thread
        local storage isn't disabled in this case, the worker thread won't see
        the token set by the thread that acquired the lock. Our assumption
        is that these cases aren't common and as such default to using
        thread local storage.
        """
        self.redis = redis
        self.name = name
        self.timeout = timeout
        self.sleep = sleep
        self.blocking = blocking
        self.blocking_timeout = blocking_timeout
        self.thread_local = bool(thread_local)
        self.local = threading.local() if self.thread_local else SimpleNamespace()
        self.local.token = None
        self.register_scripts()

    def register_scripts(self):
        cls = self.__class__
        client = self.redis
        if cls.lua_release is None:
            cls.lua_release = client.register_script(cls.LUA_RELEASE_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_extend is None:
            cls.lua_extend = client.register_script(cls.LUA_EXTEND_SCRIPT)
        if cls.lua_reacquire is None:
            cls.lua_reacquire = client.register_script(cls.LUA_REACQUIRE_SCRIPT)

    async def __aenter__(self):
        if await self.acquire():
            return self
        raise LockError("Unable to acquire lock within the time specified")

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):
        await self.release()

    async def acquire(
        self,
        blocking: Optional[bool] = None,
        blocking_timeout: Optional[float] = None,
        token: Optional[Union[str, bytes]] = None,
    ):
        """
        Use Redis to hold a shared, distributed lock named ``name``.
        Returns True once the lock is acquired.

        If ``blocking`` is False, always return immediately. If the lock
        was acquired, return True, otherwise return False.

        ``blocking_timeout`` specifies the maximum number of seconds to
        wait trying to acquire the lock.

        ``token`` specifies the token value to be used. If provided, token
        must be a bytes object or a string that can be encoded to a bytes
        object with the default encoding. If a token isn't specified, a UUID
        will be generated.
        """
        sleep = self.sleep
        if token is None:
            token = uuid.uuid1().hex.encode()
        else:
            try:
                encoder = self.redis.connection_pool.get_encoder()
            except AttributeError:
                # Cluster
                encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            token = encoder.encode(token)
        if blocking is None:
            blocking = self.blocking
        if blocking_timeout is None:
            blocking_timeout = self.blocking_timeout
        stop_trying_at = None
        if blocking_timeout is not None:
            stop_trying_at = asyncio.get_running_loop().time() + blocking_timeout
        while True:
            if await self.do_acquire(token):
                self.local.token = token
                return True
            if not blocking:
                return False
            next_try_at = asyncio.get_running_loop().time() + sleep
            if stop_trying_at is not None and next_try_at > stop_trying_at:
                return False
            await asyncio.sleep(sleep)

    async def do_acquire(self, token: Union[str, bytes]) -> bool:
        if self.timeout:
            # convert to milliseconds
            timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        else:
            timeout = None
        if await self.redis.set(self.name, token, nx=True, px=timeout):
            return True
        return False

    async def locked(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by any process, otherwise False.
        """
        return await self.redis.get(self.name) is not None

    async def owned(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if this key is locked by this lock, otherwise False.
        """
        stored_token = await self.redis.get(self.name)
        # need to always compare bytes to bytes
        # TODO: this can be simplified when the context manager is finished
        if stored_token and not isinstance(stored_token, bytes):
            try:
                encoder = self.redis.connection_pool.get_encoder()
            except AttributeError:
                # Cluster
                encoder = self.redis.get_encoder()
            stored_token = encoder.encode(stored_token)
        return self.local.token is not None and stored_token == self.local.token

    def release(self) -> Awaitable[None]:
        """Releases the already acquired lock"""
        expected_token = self.local.token
        if expected_token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot release an unlocked lock")
        self.local.token = None
        return self.do_release(expected_token)

    async def do_release(self, expected_token: bytes) -> None:
        if not bool(
            await self.lua_release(
                keys=[self.name], args=[expected_token], client=self.redis
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError("Cannot release a lock that's no longer owned")

    def extend(
        self, additional_time: float, replace_ttl: bool = False
    ) -> Awaitable[bool]:
        """
        Adds more time to an already acquired lock.

        ``additional_time`` can be specified as an integer or a float, both
        representing the number of seconds to add.

        ``replace_ttl`` if False (the default), add `additional_time` to
        the lock's existing ttl. If True, replace the lock's ttl with
        `additional_time`.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend an unlocked lock")
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot extend a lock with no timeout")
        return self.do_extend(additional_time, replace_ttl)

    async def do_extend(self, additional_time, replace_ttl) -> bool:
        additional_time = int(additional_time * 1000)
        if not bool(
            await self.lua_extend(
                keys=[self.name],
                args=[self.local.token, additional_time, replace_ttl and "1" or "0"],
                client=self.redis,
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError("Cannot extend a lock that's no longer owned")
        return True

    def reacquire(self) -> Awaitable[bool]:
        """
        Resets a TTL of an already acquired lock back to a timeout value.
        """
        if self.local.token is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot reacquire an unlocked lock")
        if self.timeout is None:
            raise LockError("Cannot reacquire a lock with no timeout")
        return self.do_reacquire()

    async def do_reacquire(self) -> bool:
        timeout = int(self.timeout * 1000)
        if not bool(
            await self.lua_reacquire(
                keys=[self.name], args=[self.local.token, timeout], client=self.redis
            )
        ):
            raise LockNotOwnedError("Cannot reacquire a lock that's no longer owned")
        return True

"Core exceptions raised by the Redis client"


class RedisError(Exception):
    pass


class ConnectionError(RedisError):
    pass


class TimeoutError(RedisError):
    pass


class AuthenticationError(ConnectionError):
    pass


class AuthorizationError(ConnectionError):
    pass


class BusyLoadingError(ConnectionError):
    pass


class InvalidResponse(RedisError):
    pass


class ResponseError(RedisError):
    pass


class DataError(RedisError):
    pass


class PubSubError(RedisError):
    pass


class WatchError(RedisError):
    pass


class NoScriptError(ResponseError):
    pass


class OutOfMemoryError(ResponseError):
    """
    Indicates the database is full. Can only occur when either:
      * Redis maxmemory-policy=noeviction
      * Redis maxmemory-policy=volatile* and there are no evictable keys

    For more information see `Memory optimization in Redis <https://redis.io/docs/management/optimization/memory-optimization/#memory-allocation>`_. # noqa
    """

    pass


class ExecAbortError(ResponseError):
    pass


class ReadOnlyError(ResponseError):
    pass


class NoPermissionError(ResponseError):
    pass


class ModuleError(ResponseError):
    pass


class LockError(RedisError, ValueError):
    "Errors acquiring or releasing a lock"
    # NOTE: For backwards compatibility, this class derives from ValueError.
    # This was originally chosen to behave like threading.Lock.

    def __init__(self, message=None, lock_name=None):
        self.message = message
        self.lock_name = lock_name


class LockNotOwnedError(LockError):
    "Error trying to extend or release a lock that is (no longer) owned"
    pass


class ChildDeadlockedError(Exception):
    "Error indicating that a child process is deadlocked after a fork()"
    pass


class AuthenticationWrongNumberOfArgsError(ResponseError):
    """
    An error to indicate that the wrong number of args
    were sent to the AUTH command
    """

    pass


class RedisClusterException(Exception):
    """
    Base exception for the RedisCluster client
    """

    pass


class ClusterError(RedisError):
    """
    Cluster errors occurred multiple times, resulting in an exhaustion of the
    command execution TTL
    """

    pass


class ClusterDownError(ClusterError, ResponseError):
    """
    Error indicated CLUSTERDOWN error received from cluster.
    By default Redis Cluster nodes stop accepting queries if they detect there
    is at least a hash slot uncovered (no available node is serving it).
    This way if the cluster is partially down (for example a range of hash
    slots are no longer covered) the entire cluster eventually becomes
